item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to disagree with the evaluations of MCD as the good-ish-er guys in this story. This entire this "part of a family replacement program initiated by Marshall, Carter and Dark in order to ensure employee loyalty;" it's a bloodless cost-benefit analysis for them, rather an action motivated by anything as base as empathy or compassion. Any "good" that comes about is incidental to the goal of having an agent not be distracted by grief. Which then kind of raises the question of whether or not it's healthier to live with a perfect simulacrum of a life, or to process the truth and eventually move on. But that goes into nature of the soul, red pill blue pill, etc. stuff that isn't really relevant to the article as such.

I dig it when people do things that appear uncharacteristically benign, only to have it be for completely selfish reasons. Jerk With A Heart Of Jerk, as the kids on TVTropes might say.

As for the ending, I'm more in line with Shaggy's evaluation. It's not a huge twist ("he sustained a massive spinal injury, but is now fine. By the way, his entire family is now in robo-suits" kind of gives the game away). The MCD-by-way-of-CI action at the end felt like it was written more as a way to get the story where the author wanted it to go than a natural evolution of the story as written. Less "oh, shit!" and more "oh, huh."

Giving it a no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This startled an upvote out of me. Well played.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I'm going to have to echo what Shaggy said above. For me, at least, this didn't go anywhere special. I can appreciate the worldbuilding in this enough, however, to walk away with a novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sort of echo here what is said here, but not quite on a scale the makes me not want to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gonna go ahead and second a lot of what Conwell said; the worldbuilding came off strong and how Anderson's character was handled was pretty spot on. Unfortunately, I was sorely disappointed with where the story went.

Though they still label them as property in official context, and apparently use them as leverage, there's something quite human and actually somewhat decent in how MC&D operates here:

They bring back a man's entire family, and allow him to return to some semblance of a normal life (up to and including the family members resuming their roles in society, not like they're being locked up and abused by cackling sadists at cocktail parties). This puts them on track to actually have the moral high ground on the SCPF for a change…which is something I can't say I've seen examined before.

Next you introduce the notion that MC&D have certain agreements and the SCPF obligations to uphold, so that once they're in containment, you could have had something occur as a result of this - that is - they could have handled the issue diplomatically, and in a way that further pushes the SCPF into the villain role, where their blind ideology is brought to the fore by friggen peddles of the macabre, no less. Instead, nothing comes of the SUSEOCT, and MC&D just goes gung-ho guns-a-blazing like a group of goofy CI nuts.

Cut to the end, where it's revealed the family man in question is himself a replicate (which was made incredibly obvious from the second paragraph of the description) and it's just matter-of-factly repeated that the SCPF should give him back…and I just can't be arsed to care about anyone by this point. This could have used, I feel, some of Jacob's perspective, or something impassioned on the part of an MC&D rep to inject some kind of pathos into the end, because as it stands now, it's just a huge letdown.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"recommended an accelerated the SCP-3613 research schedule." Just noticed this. I'm not sure the 'the' is supposed to be in there.

Also, what exactly is Hr'asm Kal, and does he know he has the caps lock on?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved it, amazing work. This gives you that mix of unease and enlightenment that I've come to associate with the best SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really dig this for a variety of reasons.

First off, it feels like a nice continuation of the relationship you established AR and MC&D having in 2987, which is great world building.

Second, I feel you do a great job with how Anderson himself would approach this request from MC&D. That initial hesitation, followed by him realizing what exactly MC&D is requesting possession bots for, and jumping on board is 100% in character.

Finally, and I'm going to hide this cause its spoliers:

+ show block
– hide block

I was blind sided by the twist ending, where Mr. Jefferson himself was a robot the whole time. Considering how a running trope in the Anderson series is people turning out to be robots all along, this really did a good job of holding the punch until the very end.

+1 Cimmerian.

And the Blade Runner reference in the title is A+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am a simple man. I see robots, I upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nicely done Cimm. Cool to see a reference to Resurrection outside the canon as well. Do you think you'd ever write for it again?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright I'll admit it I watched Blade Runner a couple times this week.

Thanks to The Great Hippo, Jacob Conwell, and Dr. Chandra for giving this draft a look before I posted it.
